title: Spring Cloud Architect Assessment
description: Architect-level evaluation focusing on Spring Cloud ecosystem for microservices: service discovery (Eureka), API Gateway (Spring Cloud Gateway), distributed configuration (Config Server), circuit breakers (Resilience4j), distributed tracing (Sleuth/Zipkin), messaging (Spring Cloud Stream), and cloud-native patterns (circuit breaker, retry, bulkhead).

questions.text: In Spring Cloud Netflix Eureka, what is the difference between a Eureka Server and a Eureka Client?
options: Eureka Server is for production; Eureka Client is for development
Eureka Server acts as service registry; Eureka Client registers itself with the server and discovers other services
Eureka Server handles load balancing; Eureka Client handles authentication
There is no difference; they are the same component
questions.explanation: Eureka Server is the registry where services register. Eureka Client (each microservice) registers itself, sends heartbeats, and discovers other services via the server.
hints: Think of a phone book: server = directory, client = person listing their number
Client sends periodic heartbeats to renew lease

questions.text: What is the purpose of @EnableDiscoveryClient vs @EnableEurekaClient in Spring Cloud?
options: @EnableEurekaClient is specific to Eureka; @EnableDiscoveryClient works with any service registry (Consul, Zookeeper, Eureka)
questions.explanation: @EnableDiscoveryClient is the generic annotation that works with any Spring Cloud Discovery implementation. @EnableEurekaClient is Eureka-specific and only works when Eureka is on classpath.
hints: One is implementation-agnostic, the other is vendor-specific
Most modern apps use the generic version

questions.text: What is the 'Self-Preservation' mode in Netflix Eureka and why is it important?
options: A security feature that encrypts service registrations
When Eureka Server stops expiring registrations if too many heartbeats fail, preventing service wipe-out during network issues
A backup mode when database is down
A performance optimization for high-traffic scenarios
questions.explanation: Self-preservation mode activates when renewal threshold drops below 85%. Eureka stops expiring instances, preventing false service unavailability during temporary network partitions.

questions.text: In Spring Cloud Gateway, what is the correct order of execution for a request?
questions.explanation: Correct order: GlobalFilters (pre) → RoutePredicate matching → Route → GlobalFilters (post). Predicates determine which route handles request; filters modify request/response.
hints: Predicates run first to find matching route
Filters wrap the request and response

questions.text: How does Spring Cloud Gateway implement circuit breaking compared to Zuul 1.x?
options: Gateway has no circuit breaking support
Gateway uses Resilience4j integration via Spring Cloud Circuit Breaker (supports reactive, non-blocking)
Gateway relies on external load balancer only
questions.explanation: Spring Cloud Gateway integrates with Resilience4j (reactive) via spring-cloud-starter-circuitbreaker-reactor-resilience4j. Zuul 1.x used Hystrix (blocking).
hints: Reactive programming model requires reactive circuit breaker

questions.text: What is the purpose of Spring Cloud Config Server's 'encryption' support?
options: To encrypt the entire configuration file at rest
To encrypt sensitive property values (e.g., passwords, API keys) using symmetric or asymmetric keys
To encrypt network traffic between Config Server and clients
To encrypt Git repository credentials only
questions.explanation: Config Server can encrypt sensitive values using {cipher} prefix. Values are decrypted before sending to clients. Supports AES symmetric and RSA asymmetric encryption.
hints: Requires JCE Unlimited Strength Jurisdiction Policy

questions.text: What is the difference between 'spring.cloud.config.uri' and 'spring.cloud.config.discovery.enabled'?
options: uri points directly to Config Server URL; discovery.enabled uses service discovery to locate Config Server by service ID
uri is for production; discovery is for development
questions.explanation: uri = hardcoded Config Server address (brittle). discovery.enabled = true allows client to find Config Server via Eureka/service registry using 'configserver' service ID.
hints: One is hardcoded, the other is dynamic
Works with service discovery for resilience

questions.text: What is the 'Circuit Breaker' pattern and which states does Resilience4j implement?
options: Three states: CLOSED (normal), OPEN (failing fast), HALF_OPEN (testing recovery)
Four states: CLOSED, OPEN, HALF_OPEN, DISABLED
Resilience4j does not implement circuit breaker
questions.explanation: Resilience4j circuit breaker has CLOSED (requests pass), OPEN (fail fast), HALF-OPEN (single test request to check recovery). Transition based on failure rate threshold.
hints: Configuration includes failure rate threshold and wait duration

questions.text: How do you configure Resilience4j retry with Spring Cloud Circuit Breaker?
options: Using resilience4j.retry.* properties and @CircuitBreaker with fallback method
questions.explanation: Resilience4j retry and circuit breaker are separate modules. Configure RetryRegistry and CircuitBreakerRegistry, or use annotations @Retry and @CircuitBreaker together.
hints: Retry tries again; circuit breaker fails fast
Often used together with different configurations

questions.text: What is the 'Bulkhead' pattern and which types does Resilience4j support?
options: Both semaphore (limit concurrent calls) and thread pool (limit concurrent threads) bulkheads
questions.explanation: Resilience4j supports SemaphoreBulkhead (limits concurrent calls, non-blocking) and ThreadPoolBulkhead (limits concurrent threads, blocking). Prevents resource exhaustion.

questions.text: What is Spring Cloud Sleuth's primary responsibility in a distributed system?
options: To add trace and span IDs to logs and headers for distributed tracing
questions.explanation: Sleuth adds a Trace ID (across whole request) and Span ID (per service) to logs and propagates them via headers. Integrates with Zipkin for visualization.
hints: Trace ID is the same across all services
Span ID changes per service hop

questions.text: How do you export Spring Cloud Sleuth traces to Zipkin?
options: Add spring-cloud-sleuth-zipkin dependency and configure zipkin.baseUrl
Sleuth automatically exports to Zipkin without configuration
questions.explanation: Add spring-cloud-sleuth-zipkin dependency. Configure spring.zipkin.baseUrl (default http://localhost:9411). Traces are sampled and sent asynchronously.
hints: Uses HTTP or RabbitMQ for transport

questions.text: What is the purpose of 'spring.cloud.sleuth.sampler.probability' configuration?
options: To set the probability of errors being logged
To set the percentage of requests to trace (e.g., 0.1 = 10%) to reduce overhead
questions.explanation: Tracing all requests adds overhead. Probability sampler (default 0.1 = 10%) randomly selects which requests to trace. Use 1.0 for 100% tracing in development.
hints: Higher value = more traces = more overhead

questions.text: What is Spring Cloud Stream's 'Binder' abstraction?
options: A way to bind HTTP requests to controllers
An abstraction over message brokers (Kafka, RabbitMQ) that provides consistent programming model
A tool for binding configuration properties
questions.explanation: Binder abstracts message broker details. Same code works with Kafka (KafkaBinder) or RabbitMQ (RabbitBinder). Only dependency and configuration changes.
hints: Write once, run on any broker

questions.text: What is the difference between @StreamListener (deprecated) and functional @Bean (Supplier/Function/Consumer) in Spring Cloud Stream?
options: @StreamListener uses annotation-based binding; functional uses Java 8+ functional interfaces with spring.cloud.stream.function.definition
questions.explanation: Functional programming model is newer (Spring Cloud Stream 3+). Define @Bean Supplier (source), Function (processor), or Consumer (sink). More testable and concise.

questions.text: What is the purpose of 'spring.cloud.stream.bindings.<bindingName>.destination' property?
options: Sets the IP address of the broker
Sets the topic/queue/exchange name in the message broker
questions.explanation: 'destination' maps to broker-specific destination: Kafka topic, RabbitMQ exchange/queue. For Kafka, binding name 'output' → destination 'orders-topic'.

questions.text: What is 'Spring Cloud LoadBalancer' and how does it differ from Netflix Ribbon?
options: Spring Cloud LoadBalancer is the reactive, non-blocking replacement for Ribbon (which is in maintenance mode)
Ribbon is reactive; LoadBalancer is blocking
questions.explanation: Ribbon is deprecated and blocking. Spring Cloud LoadBalancer is the modern, reactive client-side load balancer built on WebClient, better for non-blocking stacks.

questions.text: How do you implement custom load balancing strategy with Spring Cloud LoadBalancer?
options: Implement ReactiveLoadBalancer interface or extend RoundRobinLoadBalancer and configure via ServiceInstanceListSupplier
questions.explanation: Implement ServiceInstanceListSupplier for instance filtering/caching. Implement ReactorLoadBalancer for custom selection algorithm (weighted, least connections, etc.).

questions.text: What is the purpose of @RefreshScope in Spring Cloud Config clients?
options: To refresh the entire application context
To refresh specific @Bean or @Component when configuration changes (via /actuator/refresh)
questions.explanation: @RefreshScope marks beans whose properties can be updated without restart. POST to /actuator/refresh triggers property reload and bean re-initialization.
hints: Only beans with this annotation are updated

questions.text: What is the difference between Spring Cloud Bus and Spring Cloud Config?
options: Config Server provides configuration; Bus propagates refresh events across all instances (broadcast via message broker)
Bus is for logging; Config is for properties
questions.explanation: Config Server centralizes config. Spring Cloud Bus uses message broker (Kafka/RabbitMQ) to send refresh events to all services, avoiding individual /actuator/refresh calls.

questions.text: What is the 'Fallback' pattern in Spring Cloud Circuit Breaker and how is it implemented?
options: Using @CircuitBreaker's fallbackMethod attribute pointing to a method with same signature + Throwable parameter
questions.explanation: @CircuitBreaker(name = "serviceA", fallbackMethod = "fallback"). Fallback method must be in same class and accept Throwable as optional last parameter.
hints: Fallback runs when circuit is OPEN or method throws exception
Must return same type as original method

questions.text: What is the purpose of 'spring.cloud.gateway.discovery.locator.enabled'?
options: To enable service discovery for Gateway routes
To auto-create routes based on registered services (e.g., /SERVICE-NAME/**)
questions.explanation: When enabled, Gateway auto-creates routes for each Eureka service: /{serviceId}/**. Useful for development but requires careful security in production.

questions.text: What is the 'Time Limiter' pattern in Resilience4j?
options: Limits the total execution time of a method call
questions.explanation: TimeLimiter wraps calls with timeout. If execution exceeds timeout (e.g., 2 seconds), throws TimeoutException. Works with both synchronous and asynchronous calls.

questions.text: How do you configure Spring Cloud Gateway to route based on request headers?
questions.explanation: HeaderRoutePredicateFactory matches request headers. Example: routes: - id: admin_route predicates: - Header=X-User-Role, Admin. Supports regex patterns.

questions.text: What is the 'Retry' filter in Spring Cloud Gateway and how does it differ from Resilience4j retry?
options: Gateway's Retry filter retries failed HTTP responses (status codes); Resilience4j retries method execution including exceptions
Gateway retry is for all errors
Resilience4j retry is only for network errors
questions.explanation: Gateway's Retry filter operates on HTTP response status codes (e.g., retry on 500). Resilience4j retries on exceptions within method execution.
hints: One works at HTTP layer, one at application layer
Can combine both for different purposes
